62ec4057d6777289bcae7f22.leadin: This nom de plume was first used in the pamphlet Predictions for the Year 1708, which mocked John Partridge's astrological almanac by forecasting his imminent death. For 10 points each:
62ec4057d6777289bcae7f22.leadin_sanitized: This nom de plume was first used in the pamphlet Predictions for the Year 1708, which mocked John Partridge's astrological almanac by forecasting his imminent death. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this pseudonym which Richard Steele used as the fictitious editor for The Tatler.
This author created the character of Isaac Bickerstaff. He satirized the abuses of organized Christianity in A Tale of a Tub.
Swift attacked William Wood's coinage scheme in the first of these writings. The most radical of them is the fourth, which is addressed to "the whole people of Ireland."

646567a189865c9be6ae8c44.leadin: This artist painted portraits of Edward Grimston and a young girl wearing a blue headdress with a strap of cloth under her chin. For 10 points each:
646567a189865c9be6ae8c44.leadin_sanitized: This artist painted portraits of Edward Grimston and a young girl wearing a blue headdress with a strap of cloth under her chin. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this Early Netherlandish artist who possibly depicted St. Eligius in his painting A Goldsmith in His Shop. He is often considered a pupil of Jan van Eyck, and dominated painting in Bruges after van Eyck’s death.

The fly Christus painted at the bottom of his Portrait of a Carthusian is an early example of this technique, in which an object is depicted so realistically that it appears to be three-dimensional. Its name is French for “fool the eye.”

The swooning position of Mary in Christus’s Lamentation echoes this earlier painter’s Deposition, which was commissioned by a guild of crossbowmen. His panels depicting the Justice of Herkenbald and the Justice of Trajan for the town hall of Brussels were much admired until they were destroyed in 1695.
parts_sanitized: Name this Early Netherlandish artist who possibly depicted St. Eligius in his painting A Goldsmith in His Shop. He is often considered a pupil of Jan van Eyck, and dominated painting in Bruges after van Eyck's death.

The fly Christus painted at the bottom of his Portrait of a Carthusian is an early example of this technique, in which an object is depicted so realistically that it appears to be three-dimensional. Its name is French for "fool the eye."

The swooning position of Mary in Christus's Lamentation echoes this earlier painter's Deposition, which was commissioned by a guild of crossbowmen. His panels depicting the Justice of Herkenbald and the Justice of Trajan for the town hall of Brussels were much admired until they were destroyed in 1695.
